serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Chicken Malai Kebab is approximately 250 g (one plate of 4–5 pieces with sauce), providing around 755 kcal, 26.75 g protein, 66 g fat, and 13.1 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Malai Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (8.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.2 g/100g), and vitamin C (2.0 mg/100g) were present in the input record attributed to a secondary source; these values are retained and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plausibility. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on marinated grilled chicken with cream/dairy marinade
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Malai Kebab. GI estimated at 35 based on the low-carbohydrate profile (5.24 g/100g carbohydrate) and analogy with marinated grilled chicken dishes with dairy-based marinades, which consistently fall in the low-GI range (GI 30–40) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 5.24 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.6 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Chicken Malai Kebab; a 250 g serving delivers 2700 mg sodium, representing 135% of the 2000 mg daily limit, and should be consumed only occasionally with compensatory low-sodium meals throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low GI (~35) support glycaemic stability, but high fat content (26.4 g/100g) may delay gastric emptying and cause prolonged postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Malai Kebab is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (100–120 g) due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but the high fat content warrants post-meal glucose monitoring and portion control to avoid delayed glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.0 g/100g, trans fat 0.2 g/100g, cholesterol 85 mg/100g, and sodium 1080 mg/100g collectively present a high cardiovascular risk burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Chicken Malai Kebab to small occasional portions (≤80 g) and request preparation with yogurt only, omitting cream, ghee, and cream cheese, to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; trans fat 0.2 g/100g is pro-inflammatory; sodium 1080 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Chicken Malai Kebab in standard preparation; if consumed, request yogurt-only marinade omitting cream, ghee, and cream cheese, and limit portion to ≤80 g to minimise inflammatory fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Malai Kebab is a popular North Indian grilled chicken dish widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The dish consists of boneless chicken pieces marinated in a rich, creamy mixture of fresh cream, cream cheese, yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, and spices, then cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) or on a grill until tender and lightly charred. The name 'malai' refers to the cream or clotted cream used in the marinade, which gives the kebab its characteristic soft, moist texture and mild, subtly spiced flavour profile distinct from the more intensely spiced red tandoori chicken. Nutritionally, Chicken Malai Kebab is energy-dense at 302 kcal/100g, driven primarily by its high fat content of 26.4 g/100g (of which 8.0 g is saturated fat from cream and ghee) and moderate protein of 10.7 g/100g. Carbohydrate content is low at 5.24 g/100g with 2.85 g sugar, making it a low-GI food (estimated GI ~35). Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or fluid retention. The dish is halal and gluten-free. A standard serving in Singapore is approximately 250 g (one plate of 4–5 pieces with sauce), delivering approximately 755 kcal and 2700 mg sodium. Clinically, this dish is high-concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat content, and moderate-concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high fat slowing gastric emptying despite low carbohydrate content.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values retained from input with secondary source attribution and cross-checked for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for similar marinated grilled chicken dishes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Chicken Malai Kebab not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and no sufficiently close approved-source equivalent was available to supply defensible values. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed by HPB Foodo source; the dish is a protein-and-fat-dominant preparation with negligible plant fibre content. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar high-protein, low-carbohydrate marinated grilled chicken dishes; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Malai Kebab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (8.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.2 g/100g), and vitamin C (2.0 mg/100g) were attributed to a secondary source in the input record; these values are plausible for a cream- and ghee-based chicken preparation and are retained pending formal HPB or ICMR-NIN confirmation.
